result: The first time, stars were just stories etched into human tongues, small and flickering, but on the sixteenth rotation of the prison-planet Arinox, I watched one fall through the ceiling—a peach-pit comet trailing meteor confetti. It landed beside my cot, still sizzling, and unfolded into a map. The parchment smelled like my grandmother’s peach cobbler: cinnamon, surrender, other impossible geographies. Lines connected dots I remembered and dots I hadn’t lived yet, all labeled “back door.” Guards sleepwalked past, clocks surrendered their hands, and gravity loosened its belt. I folded the map smaller and smaller until it fit under a fingernail; I hid it there like contraband hope. Each night after lockdown I unfold it one crease at a time, tasting cobbler wax on my tongue, practicing the shape of an elsewhere. My cellmate, a poet who believes commas are tiny guillotines, whispers, “When the map is fully open, you’ll.” I nod, though I’m unsure whether I’ll escape the planet, the sentence, or merely the punctuation. Yesterday the warden installed a brighter sun to watch us better; its glare hurts the iris like abusive light. Still, one afternoon the artificial noon flickered, and in the hiccup of darkness I saw, spelled across the yard in chalky constellations, the word BEGIN. Guards erased it, but the letters linger, chalk dust settling onto tongues. Tonight the door will click open, not because locks break, but because stories learn to pick themselves. The peach pit inside me cracks; a whole orchard rolls across the sky.

It landed beside my cot, still sizzling, and unfolded into a map. The parchment smelled like my grandmother’s peach cobbler: cinnamon, surrender, other impossible geographies. Lines connected dots I remembered and dots I hadn’t lived yet, all labeled “back door.” Guards sleepwalked past, clocks surrendered their hands, and gravity loosened its belt. I folded the map smaller and smaller until it fit under a fingernail; I hid it there like contraband hope. Each night after lockdown I unfold it one crease at a time, tasting cobbler wax on my tongue, practicing the shape of an elsewhere. My cellmate, a poet who believes commas are tiny guillotines, whispers, “When the map is fully open, you’ll.” I nod, though I’m unsure whether I’ll escape the planet, the sentence, or merely the punctuation. Yesterday the warden installed a brighter sun to watch us better; its glare hurts the iris like abusive light. Still, one afternoon the artificial noon flickered, and in the hiccup of darkness I saw, spelled across the yard in chalky constellations, the word BEGIN. Guards erased it, but the letters linger, chalk dust settling onto tongues. Tonight the door will click open, not because locks break, but because stories learn to pick themselves. The peach pit inside me cracks; a whole orchard rolls across the sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 250 words.
